coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystal films via the ligand-assisted reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanan
Zhang, Canyun
Wang, Fengchao
Chen, Jin
Kong, Jinfang
Li, Lan
Sun, Yu
Xin, Yanhui
coredata.dc:description: Via the ligand-assisted reaction, highly luminescent CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystal (PNC) films were synthesized by a simple centrifugal casting process based on the supersaturated recrystallization method. The structure, the optical properties, the morphology, the stability and the potential application of the PNC films were investigated in detail. The results show that under the joint use of OA, DDAB and APTES, the obtained PNC films with 1.5 M DDAB show the strong PL emission with highest quantum yield of 77.5 %, the pure CsPbBr3 structure, the smooth surface and the enhanced stability under UV irradiation. Additionally, the white LED device based on the PNC films obtained here and a commercial red phosphor layer shows excellent optical and color properties and working stability, demonstrating the potential of the PNC films in this study in the field of light-emitting and display.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite nanocrystal films
Ligand engineering
High PLQY
Stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CsPbBr3 precursor solution assisted with ligands
Synthesis of the OA/DDAB/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 PNC films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The project was sponsored by Shanghai Sailing Program, China (No. 20YF1447600), Research start-up project of Shanghai Institute of Technology (No. YJ2021-60), Collaborative innovation project of Shanghai Institute of Technology (XTCX2023-22), and Science and Technology Talent Development Fund for Young and Middle-aged Teachers of Shanghai Institute of Technology (No. ZQ2022-6).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for O-PNC films, O/A-PNC films, O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films.

(a) PL emission spectra for O/A-PNC solution and O/D/A-PNC solution with DDAB concentrations of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M; (b), (c) and (d) are PL emission spectra, PLQY values and PL decay curves of O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films with DDAB concentrations of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M, respectively.

(a) Optical absorption and PL emission spectra for O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films with DDAB of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M, respectively; (b) Extrapolation of the band gap energy for O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films with different DDAB concentrations in Fig. 3(a).
TEM images of CsPbBr3 PNCs for O-PNC solution (a), O/A-PNC solution (b), O/D (1.5 M)-PNC solution (c) and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC solution (d).
HR-TEM images for CsPbBr3 PNCs for O-PNC solution (a), O/A-PNC solution (b), O/D (1.5 M)-PNC solution (c) and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC solution (d).
SEM images for O-PNC films (a), O/A-PNC films (b), O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films (c) and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films (d).

(a) Remaining PL intensity for O/A-PNC films and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films under continuous UV light irradiation (365 nm, 20 W) for 60 days; (b) Spectra for the white LED fabricated using O/D (1.0 M)/A-PNC films, a commercial red phosphor layer (610 nm) and a 450 nm blue chip: the inset shows a photo of the white LED working at 30 mA; (c) CIE color coordinates of the white LED in Fig. 7(b); (d) Emission spectra of the white LED at different driving currents ranging from 10 mA to 80 mA.
The list of samples and their simpler names.
The fitting parameters for the PL decay curves and the average fluorescence lifetime (τaver) for the PNC films.
The PL quantum yield (PLQY), the radiative transition rate KR, the nonradiative transition rate KNR and the KR/KNR ratio for the PNC films.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystal films via the ligand-assisted reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Hai Quan Road, Shanghai, 201418, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Sciences, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Hai Quan Road, Shanghai, 201418, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic and Engineering, North China University of Water Resources and Electric Power, Zhengzhou, 450046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: North China University of Water Resources and Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic and Engineering, North China University of Water Resources and Electric Power, Zhengzhou, 450046, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Via the ligand-assisted reaction, highly luminescent CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystal (PNC) films were synthesized by a simple centrifugal casting process based on the supersaturated recrystallization method. The structure, the optical properties, the morphology, the stability and the potential application of the PNC films were investigated in detail. The results show that under the joint use of OA, DDAB and APTES, the obtained PNC films with 1.5 M DDAB show the strong PL emission with highest quantum yield of 77.5 %, the pure CsPbBr3 structure, the smooth surface and the enhanced stability under UV irradiation. Additionally, the white LED device based on the PNC films obtained here and a commercial red phosphor layer shows excellent optical and color properties and working stability, demonstrating the potential of the PNC films in this study in the field of light-emitting and display.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fully inorganic lead halide CsPbX3(X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs) have been extensively researched due to their excellent photoelectric properties for potential applications in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [1,2], lasers [3], solar cells [4,5] and photodetectors [6]. At present, the synthesis methods of colloidal PNCs mainly include two categories, i.e., the hot injection and the supersaturated recrystallization. In the former case, the high-temperature reaction and inert gas protection are required [7], thus the operation process is relatively complicated. The latter method, generally assisted with organic ligands at room temperature, has attracted wide attention due to its simple synthesis and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the product [8].

For synthesizing the high-quality CsPbX3 PNCs, the choice of organic ligands is essential, named as ligand engineering, because the features of the ligands, including the structure, the bonding strength with PNCs, the chain length and so on, have a significant impact on the dimensionality and stoichiometry of CsPbX3 PNCs [9,10]. At the same time, the reaction mechanism between the ligands and PNCs is far from being understood clearly up to now. Compared with the traditional oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OLA) ligands with the long and straight chain, the didodecyldimethylammonium bromide (DDAB) ligand with the short and branched chain has stimulated some interests due to several advantages such as the stronger bonding strength with PNCs and its structure and component. Unlike OLA, the stronger affinity between DDAB and PNCs makes it difficult to separate from the surface of PNCs [11], resulting in the higher chemical stability of PNCs. The branched alkyl chain in DDAB can form a large spatial steric hindrance and simultaneously provide a hydrophobic layer around the surface of PNCs, which effectively improves the environmental stability of PNCs, especially moisture resistance [4]. Additionally, the bromine atoms from DDAB can fill halogen vacancies in CsPbX3 PNCs [1,12], which is very beneficial for reducing the defect density and thus improving photoluminescence performance [13].

In previous researches [14,15], our team developed a simple centrifugal casting process based on the supersaturated recrystallization to synthesize large-area high-photoluminescence CsPbX3 PNC films, with the assistance of OA, OLA and inorganic SiO2 coating. In this work, we adopted the combination of OA, DDAB and inorganic SiO2 coating without OLA to prepare CsPbBr3 PNC films in order to achieve better passivation and encapsulation effect. Simultaneously, the use of different ligands would bring different impacts on the structure and properties of PNCs, which help to achieve a deeper understanding about the reaction mechanism of ligands and PNCs. Additionally, CsPbX3 PNCs, synthesized with shorter chain ligands under the protection of SiO2 coating, have been rarely reported to date.

Cesium bromide (CsBr, 99.5 %) was purchased from Shanghai Kuling Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Lead bromide (PbBr2, 99.0 %) and Didodecyldimethylammonium bromide (DDAB, 98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %) and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, 98 %) ligand, as the precursor of SiO2 coating, were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Oleic acid (OA) was purchased from Meryer (Shanghai) Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Isopropanol (IPA, 99.5 %), as the antisolvent of PNCs, was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All reagents are analytically pure grade, and they are not purified by further experiments before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CsPbBr3 precursor solution assisted with ligands
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 2 ml OA ligand was mixed well in 20 ml DMF solvent for dissolving the sources of Cs and Pb. Then DDAB of different concentrations, 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M, 2.0 M and 2.5 M (1 M = 1 mmol), were added into the above mixed solution, which was stirred with a clean glass rod until DDAB was completely dissolved. Subsequently, 0.8 mmol CsBr and 0.8 mmol PbBr2, as the sources of Cs and Pb, respectively, were added into the above solution under magnetic stirring for 15 min until the yellow precipitates were completely dissolved. The obtained solution was laid aside for about 20 min to form the OA/DDAB assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution. The solution of the other ligand, APTES, was separately prepared just before use by a simple mixing and dilution process considering its easy hydrolysis. 1 mmol (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) was mixed well in 2 ml DMF to obtain the APTES/DMF solution with the APTES concentration of 0.5 mol/L. Finally, the OA/DDAB/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution was prepared through mixing the APTES/DMF solution of 100 μl and the above OA/DDAB assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution of 1 ml, corresponding to optimized Si: Pb molar ratio of 1.25:1. All above operations were completed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the OA/DDAB/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 PNC films
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesizing highly luminescent CsPbBr3 PNC films, the above OA/DDAB/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution of 150 μl was rapidly added into 5 ml isopropanol in a centrifugal tube to obtain the OA/DDAB/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 PNC solution based on the supersaturated recrystallization. The quartz glass substrate was ultrasonically cleaned successively with acetone, alcohol and deionized water for 15 min and then dried in air. After putting the clean glass substrate into above PNC solution, the centrifuge worked at 5000 rpm for 5 min to cast the OA/DDAB/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 PNC films. Finally the films were taken out of the solution and dried in air. The entire synthesis process was completed at room temperature in air ambient. Additionally, for comparison purpose, three other kinds of CsPbBr3 PNC films, i.e., the OA assisted CsPbBr3 PNC films, the OA/DDAB assisted CsPbBr3 PNC films and the OA/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 PNC films were also prepared by the similar centrifugal casting processing, through using the OA assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution without DDAB and APTES, the OA/DDAB assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution without APTES and the OA/APTES assisted CsPbBr3 precursor solution without DDAB, respectively. For conveniently distinguishing sample types, all samples and their simpler names used in the following text, were listed in Table 1 .

Herein, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of film samples were measured by an Ultima IV diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with a Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA). The PNC solution and films were characterized by an OmniFluo900 fluorescence spectrometer (Zolix Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd.) to obtain their PL spectra. The time-resolved PL spectra were performed with an FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) with a 450 nm excitation wavelength. The absorption curves of the PNC films were measured under a UV-3600Plus spectrophotometer (Shimfusa, Japan). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) results of PNCs were performed by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan).The morphology images of the PNC films were performed by a GeminiSEM500 field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Oxford Instruments, UK). Bruker Dimension ICON atomic force microscope (AFM) was used to characterize the surface roughness of the PNC films. All of measurements were finished at room temperature.

It is known that the structure of perovskite materials is sensitive to different chemical environments involving ligands, which significantly affects the performance of perovskite. In order to examine the influence of ligands used here on the phase structures of PNC films, we contrasted XRD patterns for four kinds of PNC films, i.e., O-PNC films, O/A-PNC films, O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films, as shown in Fig. 1 . It is found that when only OA ligand is used, O-PNC films obtained contain two kinds of phases, i.e., CsPbBr3 and CsPb2Br5, because their XRD patterns well coincide to JCPDS cards of No. 54–0752 and No. 25–0211. If OA and APTES ligands are used, O/A-PNC films show cubic CsPbBr3 phase. While OA and DDAB ligands are adopted, the CsPb2Br5 phase dominates O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films. In the case of the combination of OA, DDAB and APTES, O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films still maintain the complete cubic CsPbBr3 structure. In Fig. 1, a wide diffraction peak appears in the range from 20° to 40° for O/A-PNC films and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films due to amorphous silica [16]. The phase transformation of perovskite materials induced by ligands was reported in many previous researches [17–20]. For example, OLA ligand was supposed to play a vital role in triggering the transformation of cubic CsPbBr3 phase to the lead depleted Cs4PbBr6 phase according to the mechanism, 4CsPbBr3 Cs4PbBr6 + 3PbBr2 [18]. For the synthesis system containing DDAB ligand, it was found that DDAB could trigger a ligand-assisted exfoliation and rearrangement of the PbBr6 octahedra in CsPbBr3 to form a two dimensional (2D) CsPb2Br5 structure with large indirect bandgap and a PL inactive behavior [17]. Interestingly, besides via ligands, the CsPb2Br5 structure could also be obtained by the reaction of CsPbBr3 with excess PbBr2 based on the following mechanism, CsPbBr3 + PbBr2 CsPb2Br5 [19]. As to APTES ligand, according to related studies, it has the stronger binding ability with Pb2+ ions than OLA [21,22], so APTES could passivate Pb2+ dangling bonds on the surface of PNCs [14,15,21,22]. It is more important that the hydrolysis of APTES would form the spherical silica coating layer around PNCs [23], which is very conducive to the growth of lower-dimension CsPbX3 NCs or quantum dots (QDs).

In this study, for O-PNC films, the coexistence of little CsPb2Br5 with CsPbBr3 could result from slightly higher solubility of lead salt in the synthesis system used here, based on the above formation mechanism of CsPb2Br5 without ligands. In O/A-PNC films, the existence of pure cubic CsPbBr3 phase is also consistent with the above interaction mechanism between APTES and PNCs. For O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films, DDAB ligand would cause the phase transformation of CsPbBr3 to CsPb2Br5 by inducing the rearrangement of the PbBr6 octahedra as reported previously, leading to the dominance of the CsPb2Br5 phase compared with CsPbBr3. Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) shows that the XRD signal of the CsPb2Br5 phase becomes stronger with the increase of DDAB concentrations from 0.5 M to 2.5 M, which confirms the above opinion. In O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films, under the joint use of three ligands, OA, DDAB and APTES, the encapsulation of SiO2 coating layer around PNCs could prevent the destructive effect on the CsPbBr3 phase caused by DDAB outside PNCs, thus the cubic CsPbBr3 structure can be maintained. In addition, O/D/A-PNC films with DDAB concentrations from 0.5 M to 2.0 M were found to show similar XRD patterns with O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films in the experimental study, which verifies the above viewpoint in a certain degree.

Subsequently, we explored PL performance of the obtained PNC solution and films. The PL emission of O-PNC solution was extremely weak and rapidly quenched, and O-PNC films didn't emit light. Fig. S2 (Supporting Information) shows the PL emission of O/D-PNC solution with DDAB concentrations from 0.5 M to 2.5 M. These O/D-PNC solution samples exhibit PL emission with peak wavelengths between 507 nm and 515 nm under the excitation of a 365 nm UV light, and PL strength of O/D (1.5 M)-PNC solution reaches the maximum with a cyan color emission at 508 nm shown in the inset. However, the PL emission of these O/D-PNC solution samples quenched after two days. Furthermore, their corresponding film samples shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) had weaker emissions than the solution and quenched rapidly within a few minutes, meaning that the agglomeration or phase transformation of O/D-PNCs would happen when they accumulated together after the centrifugation. Fig. 2 shows the PL characteristics of O/D/A-PNC solution and film samples with DDAB concentrations of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M, and the PL performances of O/A-PNC solution and films without DDAB are also explored for a comparison. Fig. 2(a) shows the PL emission spectra for O/A-PNC solution and O/D/A-PNC solution with different DDAB concentrations. It can be observed from Fig. 2 (a) that O/A-PNC solution shows an emission with the peak wavelength of 511 nm. Compared with O/A-PNC solution, the PL intensity of O/D/A-PNC solution increases with the increase of DDAB concentration, and O/D/A-PNC solution sample with 1.5 M DDAB emits a dazzling cyan light of 505 nm (the inset) with the maximum PL intensity. Additionally, an obvious blue shift of the PL peaks is observed as the DDAB concentration increases due to the size effect, indicating the size of PNCs can be controlled by adjusting the DDAB concentration in the precursor solution under definite OA and APTES ligands, because the coordinating effect of the capping ligands would lead to a slower delivery rate for monomers with an increased amount of ligands [24]. However, when the DDAB concentration continues to increase to 2.0 M, the PL emission of O/D/A-PNC solution instead decreases and moves on a larger scale to 496 nm in Fig. 2(a), showing the excessive DDAB would restrain the growth of PNCs and reduce the PL emission, which was also reported previously [3]. After the centrifugal process, the PL performances of O/D/A-PNC films were studied in detail, and O/A-PNC films were also involved, as shown in Fig. 2(b). From Fig. 2(b), one can see that O/D/A-PNC films inherit the strong PL emission of their corresponding PNC solution in Fig. 2(a). The obtained films with 1.5 M DDAB emit a dazzling light (the inset photo) with the highest PL emission at 514 nm. In addition, the luminous intensity and peak position variation of O/D/A-PNC films exhibit a consistent trend with that of O/D/A-PNC solution when the DDAB concentration increases. Fig. 2 (c) shows the PLQY results of O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films shown in Fig. 2 (b). As shown in the line chart in Fig. 2(c), the PLQY value of O/A-PNC films is only 32.1 %, and the PLQY value increases with the increase of DDAB. When the amount of DDAB is 1.5 M, PLQY of the film reaches the maximum value of 77.5 %. While DDAB is 2.0 M, the PLQY value decreases to 66 %.

To explore the mechanism behind the high PLQY observed for the obtained O/D/A-PNC films, the PL decay curves for these films were measured, as shown in Fig. 2 (d). All decay curves were found to be well fitted with a triexponential decay function, and the fitting parameters were listed in Table 2 . Based on the fitted short life (τ1), medium life (τ2), and long life (τ3) in Table 2, the average lifetime (τaver) can be calculated according to Eq. (1) [25], where A1, A2 and A3 are the respective weights of three exponential functions.
(1) τaver = (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2+ A3τ3 2)/ (A1τ1 + A2τ2 + A3τ3)

On the basis of recent reports [26,27], τ1, τ2 and τ3 represent the decay lifetimes for the intrinsic exciton relaxation, the interaction between excitons and phonons, and the interaction between excitons and defects, respectively. The former is related to the radiative recombination, and the latter two are relevant with the nonradiative recombination. According to the relations of the average lifetime (τaver) and PLQY marked as Φ with the radiation transition rate (KR) and the nonradiative transition rate (KNR) [28], i.e., Eqs. (2) and (3), one can deduce Eqs. (4) and (5) to calculate KR, KNR and KR/KNR, which are shown in Table 3 .
(2) τaver = (KR + KNR)−1
(3) Φ = KR (KR + KNR)−1
(4) τaver = Φ/KR
(5) Φ = (1+KNR/KR)−1

From Eq. (5), there is no direct dependence between PLQY and the fluorescence lifetimes, but PLQY is proportional to the KR/KNR value. In Table 3, for O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films with DDAB of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M, the obtained KR/KNR values are 0.47, 0.93, 1.06, 3.45 and 1.94, respectively. With the increase of DDAB ligand, the KR/KNR ratio of the PNC films increases and exceeds 1 as DDAB is 1.0 M, meaning that the radiative transition is dominant compared with the nonradiative transition. When the DDAB amount reaches 1.5 M, the KR/KNR ratio of O/D/A-PNC films reaches the maximum of 3.45, which is supposed to mainly result from the collaborative effect of DDAB ligand and the SiO2 coating layer. That is to say, the moderate DDAB ligand encapsulated by the SiO2 coating layer could effectively reduce defect states of CsPbBr3 PNCs through filling halogen vacancies, and/or passivating surface defects probably by the strong binding between DDA+ and Br− ions [9,13], thus enhance the radiative recombination ratio (KR/KNR). When DDAB increases to 2.0 M, the KR/KNR ratio of O/D/A-PNC films decreases, probably because excessive ligand results in a smaller crystal size and more defects [3,24]. The specific mechanism behind the luminescence of O/D/A-PNC films in this study needs more efforts in the future.

Fig. 3 (a) contrasts the absorption and emission spectra for O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films with DDAB of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M. It can be observed from Fig. 3(a) that all of PNC films have an obvious absorption edge, which exhibits a blue shift trend with the increase of DDAB concentration, corresponding to the blue shift of PL emission peak from 518 nm to 504 nm, indicating that the PL emission of the PNC films results from the direct radiation recombination near the band edge [14]. Based on the absorption spectra in Fig. 3(a), the variations of (αhv)2 versus photon energy (hv) for O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films were shown in Fig. 3(b), where α, h and v stand for the absorption coefficient, Planck constant and the light frequency, respectively. In Fig. 3(b), the band gap energy values were extrapolated to be 2.38 eV, 2.39 eV, 2.40 eV, 2.42 eV and 2.45 eV for O/A-PNC films and O/D/A-PNC films with DDAB concentrations of 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 1.5 M and 2.0 M, respectively. The band gap widening of the PNC films could result from the size effect and the modified defect states with the increased DDAB.

In Figs. 4 and 5
, we investigated the morphology and structure of the PNCs in four representative solution samples, i.e., O-PNC solution, O/A-PNC solution, O/D(1.5 M)-PNC solution and O/D(1.5 M)/A-PNC solution, because the morphology and structure of PNCs in solution are closely related to that of the corresponding PNC films after centrifugation. In Fig. 4(a), in O-PNC solution, PNCs show square particles of varying sizes with a larger average size of 46.7 nm. For O/A-PNC solution in Fig. 4(b), PNCs exhibit uniform square shape and size with an average value of 23.0 nm. In O/D (1.5 M)-PNC solution in Fig. 4(c), PNCs contain some small particles (the average size of 8.6 nm) with square and rectangle shapes. Noticeably, for the case of O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC solution in Fig. 4(d), PNCs have very homogeneous square shape and size with an average size of 16.8 nm. HR-TEM images of four kinds of PNCs were shown in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and (c), the interplanar spacing analyses have identified the coexistence of CsPbBr3 and CsPb2Br5 phases for PNCs in O-PNC solution and O/D (1.5 M)-PNC solution. In Fig. 5(b) and (d), only CsPbBr3 phase has been confirmed for two cases of O/A-PNC solution and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC solution. Above observations are consistent with XRD results of the corresponding PNC films in Fig. 1.

Subsequently, we studied the surface morphology characteristics of O-PNC films, O/A-PNC films, O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films, which were prepared with above four kinds of PNC solution in Fig. 4 by the centrifugal casting method, as shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) shows that O-PNC films are formed by the accumulation of larger flaky particles with irregular shape. Across the surface of O/A-PNC films in Fig. 6(b), one can see that the films contain many small crystal particles. Fig. 6(c) shows that O/D (1.5 M)-PNC films are composed of thick lamellas, which probably belongs to the morphology of the 2D CsPb2Br5 structure shown in Fig. 1. For O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films, as shown in Fig. 6(d), it is found that crystals are wrapped in the connected silica network formed by the hydrolysis reaction of APTES and the density of the films is enhanced compared with O/A-PNC films. Concerning the PL performance, the surface roughness of O/A-PNC films and O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films is determined to be 62.5 nm and 27.9 nm, respectively, by the AFM measurements, as shown in Fig. S3 (a) and (b) (Supporting Information). This smoother surface of O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films could be conducive to the increased PLQY of the PNC films, because it may reduce the loss of the pumping light incident to a certain degree on the contact surface between the air and the PNC films, leading to the increase of the pumping light propagating in the PNC films [29].

For the practical applications, the stability of the PNC films was evaluated by continuously irradiating the samples with a UV light source (365 nm) in a darkroom in atmospheric environment at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 7 (a) . One can see that O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films can maintain 85 % of the initial PL intensity until 60 days, while the PL intensity of O/A-PNC films without DDAB ligand drops to 63 % of the initial value after 60 days, indicating that DDAB ligand and silica coating can dramatically improve the luminescence stability. Furthermore, in order to examine the application potential of the PNC films in the optoelectronic light-emitting and display field, a white LED device was fabricated by successively overlapping O/D(1.0 M)/A-PNC films and a commercial red phosphor layer (610 nm) prepared by the previous method [14] onto a blue chip (450 nm). Fig. 7(b) shows the typical emission spectrum for the white LED device and the photo of the device in dark room (inset). Additionally, the white LED working at a 30 mA current shows excellent optical and color properties, i.e., CIE color coordinates of (0.34, 0.33), a color temperature of 5284 K, a rendering index of 67.6 and a high-power efficiency of 48.69 l m/W, as shown in Fig. 7 (c). After the white LED device was continuously lighted up with a working current of 30 mA for 36 h, the EL spectra can remain stable, indicating its good working stability, as shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). By adjusting the driving currents from 10 mA to 80 mA, the emission spectrum stability of the white LED was measured in Fig. 7(d), in which the PL performances of the PNC films do not saturate under blue light below 80 mA. Above observations illustrate that the PNC films obtained in this study have great potential in the light-emitting and display devices.

In summary, in this study, a centrifugal casting method was used to prepare perovskite nanocrystal films under the assistance of three ligands, OA, DDAB and APTES. The influence of ligands on the structure, optical properties, the morphology and stability of PNC films was investigated in order to improve the PLQY and stability of perovskite films for practical applications. The results showed that as OA, DDAB and APTES were adopted together, O/D (1.5 M)/A-PNC films exhibited the highest PLQY of 77.5 %, the pure CsPbBr3 structure, the smoother surface, better irradiation stability. In the end, the white LED device with excellent optical and color properties was fabricated based on the PNC films here and a commercial red phosphor layer, demonstrating the application potential of the PNC films in light-emitting and display devices.

Yanan Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Canyun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Fengchao Wang: Resources, Project administration. Jin Chen: Software. Jinfang Kong: Validation, Formal analysis. Lan Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Yu Sun: Validation, Formal analysis. Yanhui Xin: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was sponsored by Shanghai Sailing Program, China (No. 20YF1447600), Research start-up project of Shanghai Institute of Technology (No. YJ2021-60), Collaborative innovation project of Shanghai Institute of Technology (XTCX2023-22), and Science and Technology Talent Development Fund for Young and Middle-aged Teachers of Shanghai Institute of Technology (No. ZQ2022-6).
